speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane, this is tape eight. Ruth, I'd like to ask you, after experiencing what you did, in the-- for the duration of the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --can you remember your thoughts when you did realize that the Germans had left Peterswaldau and you realized that it was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your thoughts at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think that I was-- first, the most important thing was that I responded to the hope within me. And this is that I hoped and prayed that some members of my family would have survived. Secondly, I realized, as well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I-- that there is a lot around me, a lot of joy, a lot of positiveness at that stage. Even the joyous response, when the Russian soldiers were going through the town, which people, survivors, apparently, girls, lady survivors, apparently, suffered sometimes at their hands. There was nothing like this happening with those that we have met. But I think straightaway-- maybe that's part of the nature, the human nature-- I straightaway regretted, and, sort of, I-- I realized I had certain skills, although I was already 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, I was only 17, and I really had to think very hard, what am I going to do? I was suddenly free. Now, how do you handle freedom, if you don't know what it means for six years?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have-- you didn't learn even those social skills and graces that you learn at school whilst you are doing certain things. We didn't have any of it. Now, I had to-- I had to grow up. I think I grew up in the next few days. Because I felt a lot of things were filling my body, which, let's say, I felt, you know, you can say, I could reach out and touch those feelings, which, in a way, I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: While, on the other hand, there was like a deadening, you know, a fear, when I felt I could literally-- just like when they were giving me the number, I could taste the fear, you know? And you have all thoughts coming into your head. And what if nobody did survive, you know? And then you are gripped in this fear. You didn't-- I think it was-- it was just like during the war. You really didn't know what to do. You didn't know how to handle it. So I knew, after-- after a couple of days, after two or three days, I knew, uh-uh, this is no go. This is too-- too much of a luxury. No, I give too much of a luxury. So I thought, then and later on, if I-- when I had, you know-- I felt-- I'm not saying that I didn't, because I felt hate. You know, I felt very that it happened. You know, how this could-- Then it all-- it all came out, you know? All this, you know, this-- I felt angry. I felt sad. And then I thought, oh-oh, that doesn't-- that doesn't. I'm not going to-- to fill myself with hate and bitterness.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so much hate. I don't think, because I didn't even know what it is. But bitterness, I felt the bitterness. I felt, you know, it was coming into my mouth, you know, the taste of it. But I thought, no, no, uh huh, uh huh. I-- I don't-- I don't want to do it. I don't want to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought that the way I-- you-- you were taught, this is the only thing I could go back. The way-- the certain thing I-- certain things I was taught, certain things I listened to, when I was with the girls in camp. And I thought, I don't want any negativity right now. I don't want any bitterness. How am I going to fill myself up with love? I-- I suddenly felt-- maybe not suddenly, maybe it evolved-- then a day was a very long time. After the war, a day was-- was endless. And I felt, no. I think that-- that bitterness and hate doesn't mix. And if, perhaps, I don't know if I consciously, but I knew that-- I knew that I was going to meet a lot of people. So I didn't really want to talk about it that much. I didn't want to go, and look what has happened to me, you know? Because it had happened-- it happened to the others, as well. But I told, like always, I'm going to search for something, which is-- which will allow me to put-- to-- to acknowledge who I am. Because at that stage, I knew that I am Jewish. I mean, it was shoved down my throat often enough, you know? So where other kids, when they were growing up, it wasn't. You know, with me, OK, it worked-- it worked in-- in-- in an assisting way in the long run. So I think that, again, this is when it came into focus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I know who I am. Then, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you didn't have the role-- you know, now, about the role models. And one thing we didn't have, one thing we were not aware of, that we had a-- had a poor self-image. So I really didn't-- I never sort of had a poor self-image, you know? Because the others didn't have either. When you live at such close proximity, even after the liberation, wherever it was, you went-- I went-- I decided to go back to Poland. There were hundreds, swarming, literally, you know, of survivors. They already recovered a bit. You know, they already had companies, some of them. You know, and somehow, you straightaway felt this chutzpah, you know? A bit of chutzpah came into it. A bit of davka came into it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- I think this is the way. But as I went along, and when I went back to Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Siemianowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Katowice. Well, look, I went by train. No, I went not by train. But it was like a tram, trolley, or something was running there. I don't really-- I know I went-- I walked-- it was, I walked part-- part way. I was going whatever-- whatever transport that was available.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to Bedzin. I was going to Katowice. I was going to Siemianowice. I didn't even think to go to the cemetery. I didn't-- none of it entered my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first city that you went to after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first city was Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to my grandparents' place. And everything-- everything was still like it was. I wasn't interested if it was mine, if I can sell it. I-- there was nothing even entered my mind. All I wanted, at this stage-- please try to understand-- is to see another familiar face. That's all I cared about, not money, not how I'm going to survive, not what I'm going to do, nothing, just a familiar face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find a familiar face in Bedzin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. No, I didn't. I went to Siemianowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Bedzin for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, it's hard to say. I was just-- I was going a bit there and back, not long. I went to Siemianowice. I went up to our neighbor. She was very surprised that I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pani Panczik, Panczik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-- Pani, which is lady, you know? Panczik, P-A-N-C-Z-E-K. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a huge lady. I don't remember seeing her husband. I don't remember. It's just that she-- what my mother told me, my mother went back to Siemianowice, when we went in-- which I omitted to tell you. When we were in Olkusz, my mother went, sort of, through Siemianowice, not in a legal way. Because you couldn't go there legally. Jews were not allowed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she came in there, she heard-- the first thing she heard, the 'Heil Hitler, Frau Siegreich.' So she straightaway left, you know? But she told me that she-- what she saw is Pani Panczik shifting into our flat. That's why I told this story. So Pani Panczik was in our flat. And I didn't even look at it properly. But I just went in and asked if she recognizes me. And she looked at me. And she looked at me. And after all, six years. And then I told her who I was. And she started to cry. And somehow, I didn't take to her crying. Not because that I-- my thoughts were colored by what-- what-- what my mother saw, but what I felt. That somehow, you know-- I don't know anyhow, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There was some-- she had mixed feelings, herself. And-- but then, she gave me the photos. She said, she's got something for me. And this, she gave me the photos of my two little sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of this was worthwhile for me. Whatever else I went through there, it was worthwhile. Because if it wasn't-- if I wouldn't dare to go there, I wouldn't have gotten this photo. And this photo really means the world to me. So this is how she gave me the photo. And how long did you stay in Siemianowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any familiar faces?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nobody knew anything. There was nobody that I could ask. They were all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the old people that-- old Germans. I just couldn't relate to them. I just couldn't, you know? It was not a place that filled with me with, you know, this is my home. I-- I-- I want to go back there, you know. I grew up there. It was something that became alien to me. Because then I-- then-- because when I lived there, you know, I was-- I was doing things. And my family was there. I don't think I was ready, really, to-- to face this, that maybe, you know, a thought came here and there. And it was too-- too short a time for the stability. I think that I always had a kind of a stability. But I didn't, sort of, at that time. I had to handle the feelings. and I had to find out, you know, not so much where I am going, but really to acknowledge who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you traveling with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was traveling, then, whoever came along. But, there, I went by myself. And then I went to Katowice. And I thought my cousin lived in Katowice. And I found my cousin living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your cousin's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zyga, Z-Y-G-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: --is that a male or female?

speaker: interviewee
text: Male, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zyga. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siegreich, Siegreich. Yes, Siegreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was married by that time. He met-- they were liberated in January, somewhere in Plaszow, which was close to Krakow. And he was a broken-- you know? He lived in his parents' flat. And I stayed with him for a while. And somehow, at this stage, the closest that we had before the war, somehow, I think that I coped much better than he did. I think, he's-- for his wife, his wife's sister had survived. But somehow, they were, during the war, in different environment. And somehow, it didn't straightaway gel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, somehow, the-- I don't know what it was-- but the familiarity that I remember-- he was a married man. Maybe it was my response, as well. He was only 20. But nevertheless, he was a married man, you know? And I didn't sort of-- So I stayed with them for a while. And then I left. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- I decided to go back to Germany. I thought maybe I'll go. I heard that there was some established camps, which were for the survivors. It's got a name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camps, yes. There were some camps. But when I-- so I arrived to Stuttgart in this way, going a bit here, sometimes with people, sometimes by myself. But on the way back, I already-- this took-- my going there and my getting there took quite a while. And as a matter of fact, on the way, already, I met my husband. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a very-- on a train. On a train as a matter of fact. As a matter of fact, I met him on a train really going to Poland. And he started to talk to me. He was seven years older than I was, you know? He was very kind and very gentle. And somehow, straightaway, he sort of-- it felt like he wants to take care of me, you know? But I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Motek, Mordecahi Chrzanowski. And I didn't-- I wasn't ready for it. I didn't think about any of this. But later on, we-- I said goodbye to him when we arrived. And I didn't see him for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, somehow, I met him again. And I saw him a couple of times. And then we drifted again, you know? He wasn't ready, really. I wasn't ready. He was looking. He was older, so I think he was looking at different things. He was perhaps looking if he can stay in Poland for while. His sister had survived, you know, so it was sort of different, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I said to him, that I-- that I will-- I intend to go to Stuttgart, because I heard of the DP. There could be a lot of Jewish people there, like in Muenchen. It was Ulm or now-- or Muenchen or Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I decided to Stuttgart. And he said, OK, I will come along as well, you know? So he left it behind him. And we-- and we traveled there. And he-- as a matter of fact, in Stuttgart, he found somebody that survived, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He found on top of-- of a-- a-- a-- his sister-- he was-- during the war, he was, at the age of 19 or something like that, he was briefly married for a few months. And his wife has perished, unfortunately. And-- but her father has survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow, you know, at that stage, he knew that she is not alive, but he still wanted sort of a connection with-- you know? So this is how his former father-in-law sort of, straightaway, sort of took to me. And maybe-- I don't know what had happened-- but I think he the one, really, that sort of got us really together, you know? Because, otherwise, I don't think so. We would have-- Ruth, when did you find out about your own family and what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't really know exactly what happened to them. At this stage, I mean, you couldn't go. And when you asked around-- when I went to Bedzin, as I said, I mean, I was 17 and 1/2. I really-- you know, I was not 20-odd years old. I just went there. So I just put my feelers out, tentatively. And there was nothing. Nobody knew if there were any list. As far as they knew, at the liquidation of the ghetto, they all went to Auschwitz-Treblinka-- Auschwitz-Birkenau. This is as far as-- wherever I-- if I-- if I ask this, that was their response.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you knew somebody has survived, people gravitated, somehow, you know? That was the time right after the-- a few months after the war, that you really-- you didn't know much. You know, you didn't know where you were going. You didn't know who is coming. People, again, were coming. And people were going, you know? And you felt this antisemitism. You felt this resentment. You felt this-- From who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poles, from the Poles, of course. In Bedzin, there were Poles. In the meantime, when the Jews went, the Poles took over. You know? So I couldn't-- I couldn't-- couldn't cope. Not that I couldn't cope. I wasn't ready to really-- you know? I was still hoping and praying. So it took me a couple of years, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, till I went to-- till I looked-- All together, you know, it took me quite a while before I-- I found my one cousin, Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Landsberg--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Germany. Yes. I think, Landsberg-- wait a second. If I can spell it-- if I can spell it correctly, I think it would've been, L-A-N-C-B-E-R-G, something like this, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was-- she was-- when I met her, she was already married to a wonderful young man. She was exactly two years older-- two and a half years older than I was. And, you know, it was like-- it was like an explosion, you know? Oh, she was so-- she is totally different than I was, you know? She is this-- she is so-- she knows exactly what it's at. I mean, she can-- she will enter a room, and she will assess the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's got-- on one hand, she's got-- she has the kindness and gentleness and the most lovingest-- loving-- lovely person you could meet. On the other hand, she's very like my mother's, like my father's, like my paternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: She will enter a room. And she will assess a situation, where she can, you know, the subtleties? Where she doesn't want to take advantage of the situation, but she will straightaway think how to turn it around into something positive that-- that can-- that it can make, you know? That she can-- something that you can not just feel, but that you can see. Not to hurt anybody that you can see. So she is the one that is this practical, you know this, you know? And we just get on like a house on fire, you know? Did you know her when you were growing up in Siemianowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her mother was my dad's oldest sister. And she helped in bringing my dad up, because my-- my-- my paternal grandmother was very involved in the business in Bedzin. And she was the one with the-- with the touch, you know, that she really would create an environment, a very productive environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, her mother, they lived in Radomsk, Radomsko. And this is where my grandparents had another business, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was coming, once a year or twice a year. And when she was coming, she was-- my father insisted. My father adored her, so she-- he insisted that she stayed with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was changing the clocks when she was ready to go. He said, no, another hour, another two hours, you know, things like this, where I remember. And her name was Eve-- Eva. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he absolutely adored her. And so I saw her. She was with me, two weeks, three weeks at a time, once a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she discuss with you when you met her again in Landsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just talked about girl things, you know? I mean she was married. We were just so overwhelmed that we found one-- one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there for a little while. Because I-- later, I went to Stuttgart. And if I want to think, now, she was at my-- she was at our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in-- on the 14th of June, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: So with her, I met her before the-- all-- all together, the four cousins, we all met together. Sort of, we took like two years, you know, to sort of establish this. And in what city? In Stuttgart. And we lived in-- we didn't live in the DP camps. And we lived in the Hohenheim-Strasse, Hohenheim-Strasse number 46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Stuttgart. We traveled around. We looked around. We-- my husband didn't find anybody else, but his sister was there. She lived in Stuttgart, later, as well. She lived in, not in Stuttgart, but, later on, when we got together, she met a boy. My sister-in-law met a boy in camp. And they fell in love. And after the war, they were not a girlfriend or boy-- but they just, you know, they-- when they saw each other, something there developed, a feeling. And after the war, they got married in November, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we all moved to Heidenheim. And there was, again, there was like a suburb, you can say, in Heidenheim. But there were only Jewish people, you know? You know how-- how we always have curfew, you know? So we lived all together, my sister-in-law, my brother-in-law, the two of us, and my-- and somebody else. We all lived in the same place. Did you establish a-- a religious life, again, in Heidenheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that the other people around me were not really very keen. I think that they still had to work on themselves. But I think I always-- I always sort of went in this direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just did my bit, regardless, if I heard from people around me, you know, that they-- that-- why this has happened, why that had happened. And where was-- I don't even-- I can't even say it. I can't say it, you know? So where was this? And where was the-- where was the strength from above? Where was this, you know? So I always said that it is I-- I-- that faith is a part of me. It's a living-- it's a-- it always was like a little candle within me. Nobody asked me when I would-- people like myself, it was there. It was a part of you. And that's what it was. Where did you go from Heidenheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Heidenheim, we decided to-- so we were going, really, between Heidenheim and Stuttgart. We decided to go with Aliyah Bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aliyah Bet is illegally-- there was no legal way. We decided that we want to go to Israel, to Palestine, then, the two of us. Nobody-- my husband's sister, nobody else wanted to go. But my husband was a Zionist. He felt very strongly about it. He worked, already, before the war, with various of the youth movements. So he wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as my father-- all I heard, throughout my childhood, Israel, Palestine, and you know? And he would have loved to chuck it all in and go, instead of Krakow, to go to Israel. But that was not a time, that was not-- where families sort of got separated. You had a mother, you had-- anyhow, they didn't. So how did you get in touch with this group, Aliyah Bet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- look, I-- this, I left for my husband. He got in touch. He-- He-- all of those things, till he passed away, whatever it was, if it was something illegal, if-- if it was things, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I took-- I took care of the home, of the hearth, and he took care of the mind, you know? As long-- as long as I-- as long as I could read, as I could study, as I could do things, it was all right. So he always-- he was a man that didn't-- did this not only for his family but for a lot of-- if you would ask around, people in this town, they will let you-- they would-- they would let you know what he did. Can you describe your journey to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrific, because we went to-- we literally walked to Austria. And we walked, too, at night. Then we went to some-- through some lakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody guiding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We had-- we had some guides, yes. We had some Jewish or Israeli guides. And then we went from-- also went for a while-- it was-- then, it was like a camp. There were a lot of people. I got, somewhere, a photo even from that time. And we sort of were led. And we really-- at night-- we walked only at night. And I remember, we went through some enormous hills to Milano. And we-- sort of, at night, you didn't know, because you went through some hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: You really didn't know what your next step, you know? You could have-- you could have gone really any way. You didn't know. You couldn't see in front of you. So you just prayed, you know? And you hoped that you're not going to just-- Somehow, all of us survived. And somehow, to this group, sort of nothing happened. How many people were traveling with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of people, about 50 people, 80 people. There was always-- we were always around-- surrounded by people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a group traveling with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a group traveling to Austria. And from Austria, we went to-- to Milano. And there we stayed for a few weeks. In Milano, it was in a old synagogue that we stayed, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, we stayed-- it was-- it's not to compare, really, but we were, again, maybe 100 people in a room, in this room that we stayed together. You know, by the time they were all-- most of them were couples, you know? So it was a bit awkward. And again, we didn't know what's going to happen. And-- Were you-- were you crossing borders legally or illegally?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- illegally. Everything was illegally. It was-- nothing was legal. I mean the quota, whatever was legal was filled out-- filled up. If we wanted to wait a couple of years? When you are so young, you know, a couple of years is an eternity. So we decided that we are going to go. And the others decided that they are going to go. So we just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any illegal documents with you, false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We had papers, yes. We had papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And under a false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you didn't-- I don't-- I-- I don't think-- No, I don't-- maybe. No, I really don't remember that bit again. But you know, I sort of-- I didn't retain that bit. I just know there were false paper, because I think they were under our-- they were under our name. I think they were just falsified, you know? They were not really--

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Milano, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Milano, we-- we-- I don't remember. Just-- I just know that we were told that we-- we went on a ship. And it was not really a ship. It were literally-- Proteus, I think it was called, Proteus. And we literally-- it just came to me now. We literally slept one on-- we were really sort of, you know, like-- like sardines, but this way, not standing up, but this way, one on top of the other. Because it was just-- And it-- the-- the ship wasn't a ship. It was like an old vault or something like this. And there were already girls that were pregnant, you know As a matter of fact, I met my best friends, who, later on, became my best friends in Cyprus-- I'm going ahead-- I met on this ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raisa and Mono Wurtzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. W-U-R-Z-E-L, Raisa and Mono Wurtzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, we're going to take a break there and just change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
